# Mirodan PhD Vol 2 — Master Reference for Character Voice Work

Source: Veronica Mirodan, *Yat Malmgren's Movement Psychology — A Handbook of the System*, PhD Vol 2, 1997 (printed pp. 263–575).
Synthesised: 2026-04-28 from four parallel chapter extracts (Ch I/II/III/IV).
Primary audience: Claude Opus building Laban-grounded character voice specs (Project Jin's roster).

This is reference material. It assumes the reader is past the pop summary of Laban (4 motion factors, 8 effort actions). Mirodan's framework is denser, sharper, and over-rides several of those simpler readings. Read §1 first.

---

## 1. What this changes — top 12 overrides

The simplest Laban-as-character read goes: 4 motion factors × 6 states × 8 working actions, each character is a state plus some moves. **Almost every part of that flattened reading is wrong or load-bearing in non-obvious ways.** What follows are the 12 things Mirodan actually says that override or sharpen it.

**1. "Fusions" is the canonical term for polar visitations — and the Free pole is fake.**
"You can go free if you're mostly bound, in moments" is exactly Mirodan's mechanic. Her vocabulary: *Fusions*, with the verb form *X going Y*. Definition (p. 449-450, Remote section):
> "Fusions are appearances of Free Flow covering a Bound Flow inner. A character in Fusion tries to convince others that he has Free Flow, but in reality he is extremely Bound and that is apparent to everyone except himself."
The Free pole is a mask. The Bound pole is the truth. The gap between them is visible to everyone except the character. Fusions are the dramatic device of *transparent self-deception*, not "tonal variety in the writing." See §5.

**2. Fusions only apply on the Flow axis. Other "polar visitations" don't exist.**
Strong↔Light, Direct↔Flexible, Sustained↔Quick have no Fusion equivalent in Mirodan. The introvert/extravert Subconscious Motif pairs only exist along Flow (Pressing↔Punching, Gliding↔Dabbing, Wringing↔Slashing, Floating↔Flicking — each pair shares Weight+Direction and flips on Flow). Other axes are visible properties of the body's actual behaviour; only Flow is performative-vs-actual. See §5 and §8.

**3. The character unit is six-piece, not three.**
A complete reading is `Inner Attitude × Aspect × Dominant Stress × Outer Action(s) × Lining × Working Actions`. Naming "Adream" is doing 1/6 of the work. Naming "Adream-Strong-Bound" is doing 2/6. Mirodan's full readings always hit all six. See §2.

**4. Mobile, Awake, and Remote can never be characters. They are Action Attitudes only.**
> "There are no genuinely Mobile characters" (p. 567) — and the same applies to Remote (p. 432) and Awake (p. 474). They lack Intending (Weight) and therefore can't be embodied as a baseline. They exist only as outer projections of Stable, Adream, or Near baselines under stress. Use them for moments of overwhelm (Mobile), spiritual or grief withdrawal (Remote), or cold awareness (Awake) — never as homes. See §3.

**5. The three opposite-pairs are mutually exclusive and absolute.**
Stable ↔ Mobile. Near ↔ Remote. Awake ↔ Adream. A character anchored at one pole CANNOT have its opposite as either Inner Attitude or Action Attitude. The strongest hard rule in the system. (See p. 503-504, p. 432, p. 474.) When Cordelia and Lear "change places" or Creon "breaks down into Mobility," that's the polar dam breaking — narrative.

**6. "Shadow Moves" are 2-axis fragments that betray hidden motivation. They are NOT axis-pairs.**
Earlier readings (including the simpler PDF tables) collapsed Shadow Moves with axis-cocktails like "Strong & Direct." Wrong. Per Mirodan (pp. 354-356), Shadow Moves are involuntary, unconscious gestures (twitches, head-scratches, wrist flicks) that are *deficient in one Motion Factor* — therefore 2-axis, not 3-axis. Crucially: "Shadow Moves always reveal the hidden nature and never the acknowledged intentions of the character" (p. 357). Paulina's verbal "to be loved" / Shadow says "to control." See §9.

**7. Working Actions have a mandatory preparation hierarchy.**
> "We cannot carry out a Punching action without first undergoing the inner preparation of Pressing." (p. 347)
The four Quick (extravert) Working Actions each require their Sustained (introvert) partner as preparation:
- Punching needs Pressing-prep
- Slashing needs Wringing-prep
- Dabbing needs Gliding-prep
- Flicking needs Floating-prep
Skip the prep, the action degrades to its prep-version. For per-line audit: a Punch-open ("NO at 46.") with no prep before it isn't actually a Punch — it's a Press. Hard-exit Punches work because the whole post is the prep. See §8.

**8. Tempo (Time/Deciding) is *primum mobile*.**
Deciding has no toward/away valence like the other three Inner Participations (Intending, Attending, Adapting). It pre-decides which function engages with the Objective. "Tempo itself, being a function of Time, is the physical realization of the Inner Participation of Deciding" (p. 286). Tempo is the engine, not just an axis. Don't treat the four functions as symmetric.

**9. Stress is BOTH a baseline trait AND episodic.**
Each character has a *dominant* Stress (Big Daddy = Flow-dominant; Cleopatra = Space-dominant; Lear = Time-dominant), but BOTH stresses available to the character can switch within a scene. "Characters can switch between them within scenes, to create different relationships and different 'beats'" (p. 470). Mirodan's vocabulary: *the swing of the pendulum.* See §4.

**10. Stress carries a status grammar.**
> "the more developed (more mature or higher in status) a character is, the more likely it is that it will have a Space-stress. Less developed Near characters are Flow-stressed, while Adream characters which are lower either in intelligence or in social position are Time-stressed." (p. 458 footnote)
Time-stress = adolescent / lower. Space-stress = mature / higher. Flow-stress = emotional / sociable. Read the stress as a class/status indicator, not just a mechanical axis.

**11. Negatives are not "axis off." They are *frozen interplay*.**
Heavy doesn't mean Weight-absent. It means "aware should be Strong but cannot mobilise" (p. 334) — the interplay between Light and Strong is suspended in static equilibrium between poles. Same for Adrift, Indecisive, Irrelated. Each Negative is a paralysis on its axis, not an absence. See §10.

**12. Stress IS the third Motion Factor that completes the visible Action.**
Inner Attitudes use 2 Motion Factors → Shadow Moves (subliminal). Action Attitudes / Externalized Drives use 3 Motion Factors → full Working Actions (visible). The Stress is the bridge — the third axis added to the inner pair to produce a visible 3-axis action. (p. 530.) "Inner Attitude + Stress → Action Attitude" is the architecture link.

Bonus: *Vision is omnipresent catalyst.* Even the three Weight-bearing Attitudes (Stable, Near, Adream — nominally Visionless) carry Vision as catalyst because every character has a Super-Objective and every Super-Objective is essentially visionary. (p. 540.) Vision is the white-light prerequisite, like Time, not just a fourth peer Drive.

---

## 2. The Six-Piece Character Unit

A character spec that names only an Inner Attitude is doing about 1/6 of the work. The full unit, per Mirodan:

```
1. Inner Attitude         — which 2 of 4 Motion Factors are active in baseline
                              (Stable, Near, Adream — only these can be characters;
                               Mobile, Remote, Awake are Action Attitudes only)

2. Aspect                 — which Inner Participation is emphasized:
                              Intending → enclosing
                              Attending → penetrating
                              Deciding → circumscribing
                              Adapting → radiating
                              FIXED per character; determines psychological type.

3. Dominant Stress        — which of the two Motion Factors NOT in baseline acts as
                              primary stressor. Pole specified (Bound vs Free, etc.).
                              Mostly fixed; both Stresses available, dominant emerges most.

4. Outer Action Attitudes — 1-2 of the OTHER states (Mobile/Awake/Remote and/or
                              opposite-baseline-states) that the baseline + stress
                              produces as visible projections. Deterministic from 1+3.

5. Lining                 — the secret driver beneath the visible Outer Action.
                              The Inner Action that the Outer covers/lies-about.
                              Outer/Lining gap is the engine of dramatic life.

6. Working Actions        — the 1-3 named gestures (Pressing, Punching, Wringing,
                              Slashing, Dabbing, Gliding, Flicking, Floating) the
                              character defaults to. Concrete, actor-direction-quality.
                              Bound by prep hierarchy.
```

Worked example (Hawthorne, *The Crucible*, Mirodan p. 565):
1. Inner Attitude: **Stable**
2. Aspect: **Enclosing** (Intending emphasized)
3. Dominant Stress: **Time-stress more than Flow-stress** ("he has no Adapting, remains always firm")
4. Outer Action Attitudes: **Near and Awake**
5. Lining: Stable inner (Sustained/Direct, "certainty") underneath the Near surface — the Stability that makes the Near brutal
6. Working Actions: **Pressing and Gliding** ("his working Actions are mostly Pressing and Gliding")

Plus: Drives axis = **Doing/Spell** with **Doing-Passion Action axis** (Stable Diagram A).

That's a complete spec. The character can be staged, voiced, edited line-by-line against it.

---

## 3. The Six Inner Attitudes — full reference

### 3.1 STABLE — "unadapting" (the intelligent ruler)

**Inner factors**: Intending + Attending (Weight + Space) — Sensing + Thinking (p. 487).

**Stresses available**: Time or Flow.

**Outer Action map**:
- Time-stressed → Near (Time+Weight) or Awake (Time+Space)
- Flow-stressed → Adream (Flow+Weight) or Remote (Flow+Space)

**Aspects**:
- **Enclosing** (Intending emphasized) — Hawthorne (Crucible), Solness, the establishment patriarchs
- **Penetrating** (Attending emphasized) — the Duke (Measure for Measure), inventors, intellectuals

**Variations** (Weight × Space, no Fusions because no Flow axis):

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs | Outer Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strong/Direct | **Commanding** | Punching/Pressing | Commanding demonstration or acceptance of a 'bold resolve' |
| Light/Flexible | **Receptive** | Floating/Flicking | Receptive acceptance or rejection of a 'welcoming tenderness' |
| Strong/Flexible | **Practical** | Wringing/Slashing | Developing intention to cast or to submit to a 'spell-binding power' |
| Light/Direct | **Self-Contained** | Gliding/Dabbing | Cautious expression or acceptance of a 'gentle deference' |

**Feel** (pp. 487-493):
> "Stable characters appear in control of events and, by extension, are considered to have an elevated social or spiritual status. They are, says Yat Malmgren, like a powerful car, a Bentley or a Rolls-Royce: a lot of power contained under a smooth exterior and always giving the impression of knowing exactly where it is going." (p. 488)

Stable is generated by **moving away from Mobility**: "Stable can therefore be said to consist of a lack of Mobility... a character needs to have experienced and dismissed the sensuous and emotional excitements characteristic of youth" (p. 489). Discarding Mobility = devoid of Flow and Time. "All important decisions have already been taken and it is only a matter of pursuing the chosen course to the end."

Imagery: oak trees, elephants, pyramids, moose alone in icy wasteland. Free-standing.

Mirodan recommends "**unchangeable**" as Stable's true characteristic — past adapting that ended all subsequent adaptation.

**Time-stressed Stable** (Creon, Hawthorne) → enclosing, brutal, Near + Awake outers. "Rock-like Stable."

**Flow-stressed Stable** (Werle, the Duke) → softer, "almost feminine, flexible, yielding, introvert" Adream + Remote outers (p. 491fn).

**Negatives**: Heavy + Adrift (Stable contains Weight + Space).

**Drive axis**: Doing + Spell (Diagram A: Doing/Spell with Doing-Passion axis; Diagram D: Spell/Doing with Spell-Vision axis).

**Stable is structurally singular in a cast** (p. 575): "It would be difficult to find a Stable triangle or square, because it would be most unusual to have more than one Stable Character in a play." Plays with two Stables would lack productive antagonism.

### 3.2 ADREAM — "unawareness" (the lover)

**Inner factors**: Intending + Adapting (Weight + Flow) — Sensing + Feeling (p. 453).

**Stresses available**: Time or Space.

**Outer Action map**:
- Time-stressed → Near (Weight+Time) or Mobile (Flow+Time) — *the "split" mechanic*
- Space-stressed → Stable (Weight+Space) or Remote (Flow+Space)

**Aspects**:
- **Enclosing** (Intending dominant) — Mimi, Cho-Cho-San, **Othello, Hedda Gabler, Claudius, Stanley** — sensuous
- **Radiating** (Adapting dominant) — Desdemona, **Hamlet, Ophelia, Richard II, Feste, Orsino, Antonio, Jaques, Sebastian, Viola, Blanche, Miss Julie** — feeling type

**Variations** (Weight × Flow, Fusions present):

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs | Outer Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strong/Bound | **Sombre** | Punching/Pressing | Overpowering sombre awareness/unawareness of a 'staunch resolve' |
| Light/Free | **Irradiant** | Floating/Flicking | Irradiant unfolding or enfolding of a 'sympathetic exultation' |
| Strong/Free | **Overpowering** | Wringing/Slashing | Irradiant intention of casting a spell or being spell-bound |
| Light/Bound | **Diffused** | Gliding/Dabbing | Diffused sensation of a 'welcome feeling' or 'irreconcilable feeling' |

**Fusions** (p. 469):
- Strong/Bound going Strong/Free → **Pressing going Slashing** (the adolescent rage held in, releasing as door-slam / slap)
- Light/Bound going Light/Free → **Gliding going Flicking** (teen giggle, actor corpsing, La Ronde wife)

**Feel** (pp. 453-460):
> "In Adream, the Character's Intending combines with Adapting (Feeling). As a result the Character's Intending (desires, Objectives and dynamic quality) is buffeted by the storm of his emotions. Yat Malmgren likens the Character to a car driving to a destination — Adream characters career wildly from one side of the road to another, prey to their emotions." (p. 454)

Adream is composite: Near + Mobile. "Sensing and Feeling are more often than not in conflict... Adream characters are often torn between two contradictory impulses — one can almost say that they have a dual nature" (p. 454).

> "It is doubly 'unaware': Adream characters are not only unaware of anything around them, but neither are they interested in becoming aware. ... For Adream characters ignorance is bliss." (p. 455)

**The Adream split** (p. 456): A Time-stress causes Adream to *split* into its component Near + Mobile simple Attitudes — these become the outer Action Attitudes. Time-stressed Adream therefore "denies itself the possibility of Stable and Remote Actions, thus giving up on vision, on ideas." Space-stressed Adream consolidates: Stable + Remote outers, "outer appearance of intelligence, control and aristocratic or intellectual aloofness."

**Maturity gradient** (p. 458fn): Time-stress = adolescent / lower status; Space-stress = mature / higher status.

**Negatives**: Heavy + Irrelated (Adream contains Weight + Flow).

**Drive axes** (4 sub-diagrams):
- A: Space-stressed Enclosing — Spell/Passion, Spell-Doing axis (Claudius)
- B: Space-stressed Radiating — Spell/Passion, Spell-Vision axis (Hamlet, Hedda, Miss Julie)
- C: Time-stressed Enclosing — Passion/Spell, Passion-Doing axis (Gertrude, Stanley, Lear, Jean)
- D: Time-stressed Radiating — Passion/Spell, Passion-Vision axis (Ophelia, Lovborg, Blanche)

### 3.3 NEAR — "relating" (down to earth)

**Inner factors**: Intending + Deciding (Weight + Time) — Sensing + Intuiting (p. 409).

**Stresses available**: Space or Flow.

**Outer Action map**:
- Space-stressed → Stable (Space+Weight) or Awake (Space+Time)
- Flow-stressed → Adream (Flow+Weight) or Mobile (Flow+Time)

**Aspects**:
- **Enclosing** (Intending dominant) — Big Daddy, Juno, Captain Boyle, Brecht's workers, peasants, Sir Toby, Rebecca Nurse — earthy, sensuous, weighty
- **Circumscribing** (Deciding dominant) — Casaubon, Mrs Elvsted, the Nurse in R&J, Iago (if Near), Tesman, Maria, Feste, Christine, soldiers, clerks, Columbo — conventional/imitative

**Variations** (Weight × Time, no Fusions):

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs | Outer Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strong/Quick | **Materialistic** | Punching/Slashing | Sudden aggressive intention towards or away from a 'materialistic desire' |
| Light/Sustained | **Human** | Floating/Gliding | Slow decision to express or reject a 'gentle tenderness' |
| Strong/Sustained | **Warm** | Pressing/Wringing | Staunch intention of 'warm consent' or 'warm dissent' |
| Light/Quick | **Cool** | Dabbing/Flicking | Sudden pert intention of 'intimacy' or 'estrangement' |

**Feel** (pp. 410-417):
> "Near characters react to the world through their senses. They continuously take decisions about their sensations: they place intuitively every new sensation in a 'box' alongside other, similar experiences classified in neat mental drawers and files." (p. 410)

Near = "intuitive decisions taken about sensation" — common sense. "Blinkered — they can only see what is in front of their noses" (p. 414). "A spade's a spade." Sees objects, not symbols.

Posture: width, horizontal axis. Goose-like rolling gait. Heat-radiator metaphor — "cool" to "hot" range (Faye Dunaway cool, Mel Gibson hot).

**Class is not predetermined**: "Yat Malmgren reminds his students that Near does not have to be low class" (p. 563). Maria, Feste are Near and high-class.

Greek chorus = "ultimate example of a Near relationship... 'one voice', the 'voice of common sense'" (p. 422).

**Negatives**: Heavy + Indecisive (Near contains Weight + Time).

**Drive axes**: Near is generated by **Doing + Passion** (the two Drives that share Near as their meeting-point). The four Diagrams (A/B/C/D for Near) split on Stress×Aspect.

### 3.4 MOBILE — "adapting" (highly emotional) — STATE ONLY, NOT A CHARACTER

**Inner factors**: Adapting + Deciding (Flow + Time) — Feeling + Intuiting (p. 503).

**Critical**: "There are no genuinely Mobile characters" (p. 567). Mobile lacks Intending and therefore cannot be embodied. Mobile exists only as an **Action Attitude** of Adream (Time-stressed) or Near (Flow-stressed) characters.

**Stresses (when Mobile arises as outer)**: Weight or Space.

**Aspects**:
- **Radiating** (Adapting dominant) — lived emotion, "warm glow"
- **Circumscribing** (Deciding dominant) — displayed/affected emotion

**Variations** (Time × Flow, Fusions present):

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs |
|---|---|---|
| Quick/Bound | **Unacknowledged** | Punching/Slashing |
| Sustained/Free | **Acknowledged** | Floating/Gliding |
| Quick/Free | **Revealed** | Dabbing/Flicking |
| Sustained/Bound | **Concealed** | Pressing/Wringing |

**Fusions**:
- Quick/Bound going Quick/Free → Slashing going Dabbing (Richard II "playing puzzled")
- Sustained/Bound going Sustained/Free → Wringing going Gliding (frozen smile over guilt)

**Feel** (pp. 504-513):
> "Mobile is a combination of emotion and intuition. As it has no Weight, it is a state, not a character who can be embodied physically on stage. However, because its essence is unadulterated, overwhelming emotion, it is one of the most rewarding, and often the most sought after, of acting states." (p. 504)

> "In Mobile, Yat Malmgren says, Intending is thrust out of the Inner Attitude by the centrifugal force of the emotion. As a result, a character in a Mobile state aspires towards outside Weight, clings to Weight like ivy to the 'Near' oak tree, defends itself with Weight on the Outer Action." (p. 508)

Mobile attracts to water (Ophelia, Abigail). In crowds: Bachantae frenzy, mob in *Enemy of the People*. "An entire nation can become Mobile" — the 1980s Romanian Hamlet courtiers under Ceausescu (p. 567).

**Negatives**: Indecisive + Irrelated.

**When Mobile fires**: terror, ego-disintegration, ecstatic release, mass overwhelm, Cordelia/Lear-style polar collapse, end-of-play breakdown.

### 3.5 REMOTE — "solitude" (ascetic) — STATE ONLY, NOT A CHARACTER

**Inner factors**: Attending + Adapting (Space + Flow) — Thinking + Feeling (p. 432).

Critical: Remote can't be a baseline character — "We therefore speak of Remote 'states' or 'tempi', not of Remote Inner Attitudes or Characters" (p. 432). Remote arises only as an Action Attitude of **Adream (Space-stressed)** or **Stable (Flow-stressed)** characters.

**Stresses (when Remote arises as outer)**: Weight or Time.

**Aspects**:
- **Penetrating** (Attending dominant) — Thinking type (Dombey, the cold calculator)
- **Radiating** (Adapting dominant) — Feeling type (Frankford, the spiritual surrenderer)

**Variations** (Space × Flow, Fusions present):

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs | Outer Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct/Bound | **Egocentric** | Punching/Pressing | Narrowing egocentric withdrawal into or rejection of an 'unsociable solitude' |
| Flexible/Free | **Altruistic** | Floating/Flicking | Embracing altruistic feeling for or against a 'sociable cordiality' |
| Direct/Free | **Sociable** | Gliding/Dabbing | Developing or contracting feeling of 'sociable companionship' |
| Flexible/Bound | **Unsociable** | Wringing/Slashing | Growing reflective image of a 'welcome solitude' or 'unwelcome solitude' |

**Fusions** (p. 432, p. 449-450):
- Direct/Bound going Direct/Free → **Pressing going Dabbing** ("self-obsessed egocentric making visible efforts to be pleasant" — Greenfield in *Maltese Falcon*)
- Flexible/Bound going Flexible/Free → **Wringing going Flicking** ("society hostess eaten inside with resentment but covering badly with casual flicks of ash, opening fan, twitches")

**Feel** (pp. 433-442):
> "Remote does not concern itself with physical realities — it deals only with ideas and problems and these are intangible." (p. 433)

> "The actor seeking Remote must find an intellectual concept which can produce emotion without having recourse to emotional memory — because emotional memory operates through the senses, and 'Sensing is inimical to the very essence of Remote'." (p. 432)

> "Remote ideas are not to be confused with reactions to personal hurt — 'my girlfriend left me' is not a Remote-inducing idea. Remote is induced by ideas of the philosophical, political or religious type." (p. 432)

Voice: "hollow, weightless, colourless — it has the disembodied resonance of the tomb" (p. 433).

Image: vacuum in the kinesphere. Nietzsche: "what is creative does not take place in the market place. In the market place the fleas bite you" (p. 441).

**Religious technology**: pp. 569-570 stretch is a quiet override — Remote is the Inner Attitude (state) of Christian martyrs, mystics (St John of the Cross, Santa Teresa, Ignatius), Nero's Christians, charismatic congregations. Not just emotional withdrawal — *spiritual* withdrawal.

**Negatives**: Adrift + Irrelated.

**When Remote fires**: surgical kill against an irritating crowd, philosophical/religious solitude, grief withdrawal, sarcasm-as-distance (Hamlet's "To be or not to be"; Jimmy Porter; Nigel's FISA register).

### 3.6 AWAKE — "awareness" (the intellectual) — STATE ONLY, NOT A CHARACTER

**Inner factors**: Attending + Deciding (Space + Time) — Thinking + Intuiting (p. 474).

Critical: Awake can't be a baseline. Mirodan: "Awake (like Mobile and Remote), not having Intending as a component, can only exist as an Action Attitude... Awake only appears as a component of the Action of either a Stable (Time-stressed) or a Near (Space-stressed) character" (p. 474).

**Stresses (when Awake arises)**: Weight or Flow.

**Aspects**:
- **Penetrating** (Attending dominant) — Sherlock Holmes, Brother William, Talleyrand — "see-through" intellect
- **Circumscribing** (Deciding dominant) — Stoppard's George, Columbo (Near faking Awake) — thought as cover

**Variations** (Space × Time, no Fusions):

| Variation | Label | Subconscious Motifs | Outer Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct/Quick | **Acute** | Punching/Dabbing | Acute sudden decision in favour of or against an 'idea' |
| Flexible/Sustained | **Doubting** | Floating/Wringing | Slowly dawning attention towards or away from a 'doubting awareness' |
| Direct/Sustained | **Certain** | Pressing/Gliding | Slow dawning of a 'certain' or 'uncertain awareness' |
| Flexible/Quick | **Uncertain** | Flicking/Slashing | Sudden embracing image of a 'new idea' or 'new problem' |

**Feel** (pp. 474-479):
> "Awake is the name given by Carpenter to pure intellectual ability. Awake deals with ideas — it is pure thinking in action... characters with Awake ability are master logicians, their thinking does not drift towards the intuitive. On the contrary, they deal mainly with facts." (p. 474)

> "Awake is solid, static, light and transparent: like glass, like crystal, like diamonds. Awake eliminates both the sensuous and the emotional responses aroused by the objective world and replaces them with thought. This is why characters in Awake mode give the feeling of being sensuously cold and emotionally neutral." (p. 475)

**Acceptance = Awake** (p. 572): "Yat Malmgren considers that acceptance (Job-like) is an Awake attitude." Brecht's consenters at the moment of sacrifice are Awake D — *clear-eyed surrender*, not collapse. Counter-intuitive but deliberate.

**Negatives**: Adrift + Indecisive.

---

## 4. Stress mechanics — full treatment

### 4.1 The deterministic mapping

Every Inner Attitude that can be a character (Stable, Adream, Near) has TWO available Stress axes — drawn from the two Motion Factors NOT in its inner pair. Each Stress, combined with one of the two inner factors, produces an Outer Action Attitude.

Three complete tables:

```
Stable inner = Weight + Space
TIME + WEIGHT = NEAR        AWAKE = TIME + SPACE
FLOW + WEIGHT = ADREAM      REMOTE = FLOW + SPACE

Adream inner = Weight + Flow
WEIGHT + TIME = NEAR        STABLE = WEIGHT + SPACE   (Space-stress only)
FLOW + TIME = MOBILE        REMOTE = FLOW + SPACE     (Space-stress only)

Near inner = Weight + Time
SPACE + WEIGHT = STABLE     AWAKE = SPACE + TIME
FLOW + WEIGHT = ADREAM      MOBILE = FLOW + TIME
```

So a Time-stressed Stable can outer as Near or Awake. Flow-stressed Stable as Adream or Remote. Same logic for Adream and Near.

### 4.2 Pole specification

Stress is not just "Flow" — it's specifically Bound-Flow or Free-Flow. Same for Time (Quick or Sustained), Weight (Strong or Light), Space (Direct or Flexible). Werle is *Bound-Flow-stressed* Stable; Big Daddy is Free-Flow Near. The pole determines the Outer's Variation.

### 4.3 Stress is BOTH dominant trait AND episodic

Each character has a *dominant* Stress (mostly fixed). But the non-dominant Stress remains available and emerges in scene-moments to create "beats." Mirodan's Lear example (pp. 470-473): mid-scene Time-stress→Space-stress migration. The "swing of the pendulum."

> "Characters can switch between them within scenes, to create different relationships and different 'beats'." (p. 470)

### 4.4 Status grammar

| Stress | Reads as |
|---|---|
| Time-stress | adolescent / lower status / immature |
| Space-stress | mature / higher status / sophisticated |
| Flow-stress | emotional / sociable |
| Weight-stress | (only for Mobile/Remote/Awake outers) |

Per-Attitude: Time-stressed Adream is youthful (Ophelia); Space-stressed Adream is mature (Hamlet, Brack, Hedda).

### 4.5 The Adream split — special mechanic

Time-stressed Adream literally *fragments* to its constituents Near + Mobile. Space-stressed Adream consolidates to unified Stable + Remote. (p. 456.) Same Inner Attitude, opposite outer projection profile.

### 4.6 The fundamental hard rule

Three pairs are mutually exclusive — not transitionable, not co-existable:

```
Stable  ↔  Mobile
Near    ↔  Remote
Awake   ↔  Adream
```

A character cannot be Stable AND Mobile, etc. "Polar flips" (Cordelia/Lear, Creon's collapse) are the *dam breaking* — narrative pivots, not steady states.

### 4.7 Stress as the third Motion Factor

> "The Externalized Drives are expressed 'fully' through three Motion Factors creating complete Working Actions. The Inner Attitudes, on the other hand are only expressed through two 'incomplete' Motion Factors, in the form of Shadow Moves." (p. 530)

So: Inner Attitude = 2 factors → Shadow Moves (subliminal). Action Attitude / Externalized Drive = 3 factors → full Working Action (visible). Stress is the bridge.

---

## 5. Fusions — the polar visitation mechanic

### 5.1 Definition

Mirodan's vocabulary: *Fusions*, with verb form *X going Y*.

> "Fusions are appearances of Free Flow covering a Bound Flow inner. A character in Fusion tries to convince others that he has Free Flow, but in reality he is extremely Bound and that is apparent to everyone except himself." (p. 449-450)

The Free pole is fake. The Bound pole is the truth. The gap is *transparent self-deception* — visible to audience and other characters as a fake.

### 5.2 Scope: only Flow-Attitudes

Fusions are documented only for Adream, Mobile, and Remote — the three Inner Attitudes containing Flow. NOT for Stable, Near, or Awake (those have Time/Space/Weight stresses, not Flow). The Bound↔Free axis is the only documented polar-visitation axis.

The other axes (Light↔Strong, Direct↔Flexible, Sustained↔Quick) have no Fusion equivalent because the introvert/extravert Subconscious Motif pairs only exist on Flow:

```
Strong/Direct/Bound (Pressing) ↔ Strong/Direct/Free (Punching)
Light/Direct/Bound (Gliding)   ↔ Light/Direct/Free (Dabbing)
Strong/Flex/Bound (Wringing)   ↔ Strong/Flex/Free (Slashing)
Light/Flex/Bound (Floating)    ↔ Light/Flex/Free (Flicking)
```

Each pair shares Weight + Direction; only Flow flips. There's no equivalent "introvert Strong / extravert Strong" pair. Strong is just Strong.

### 5.3 The release-valve logic

Mirodan's adolescent example (p. 469):
> "the 'introvert' Subconscious Motif of Strong/Bound is Pressing, and the 'extravert' Subconscious Motif of Strong/Free is Slashing. Therefore, Strong/Bound going Strong/Free will manifest itself as Pressing going Slashing."

> "That is why, says Yat Malmgren, adolescents slam doors and stamp their feet, hit their fists on the table or slap rivals (or the equivalent actions in words) — these gestures are outer Slashing moves motivated by an inner Pressing. The adolescent-like character is angry, but at the bottom of his anger there is fear and a search for self-preservation. If he were to remain in his 'sombre' (Strong/Bound) mode and act with violent gestures, real Punches based on inner Pressing, he could kill in anger. This is socially unacceptable and dangerous, so such characters weaken the power of the anger through outer Free Flow, turning the Punch into a slap (Slashing)."

So Bound→Free is the *socially-permitted release of dangerous truth*. The energy that can't be expressed Bound (would kill) gets redirected through Free flow as a slap.

### 5.4 The four documented Fusions per Flow-Attitude

| Inner Attitude | Variation Fusion | Inner Motif → Outer Motif | Mirodan example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Adream | Strong/Bound → Strong/Free | Pressing → Slashing | Adolescent slam, hit-fist, slap rivals (p. 469) |
| Adream | Light/Bound → Light/Free | Gliding → Flicking | Teen giggle, actor corpsing, La Ronde wife (p. 469-470) |
| Remote | Direct/Bound → Direct/Free | Pressing → Dabbing | "Self-obsessed egocentric making visible efforts to be pleasant"; Greenfield in Maltese Falcon (p. 450) |
| Remote | Flexible/Bound → Flexible/Free | Wringing → Flicking | "Society hostess eaten inside with resentment but covering badly with casual flicks of ash, opening fan, twitches" (p. 450) |
| Mobile | Quick/Bound → Quick/Free | Slashing → Dabbing | Richard II "playing puzzled" (p. 522) |
| Mobile | Sustained/Bound → Sustained/Free | Wringing → Gliding | Frozen broad smile covering guilt (p. 522) |

### 5.5 Triggers, duration, returns

**Triggers**: social impossibility of staying Bound (would-kill anger → slap), dual emotional pressure (La Ronde wife: attraction + insecurity), revealed feeling that can't be sustained inwardly (teen giggle, actor corpsing).

**Duration**: episodic, not state change.
> "Carpenter is clear that the Mobility lies in the moment of revelation, not in the enjoyment which precedes and follows it" (p. 497).

The Fusion is a moment of release. The character returns to Bound baseline afterward. The verb "going" carries kinetic, transient sense.

**Returns**: implicit. The Free is dissipation, the Bound reasserts. Energy released, inner pole resumes.

### 5.6 Signature, not drift

Mirodan treats Fusions as *part of the character's repertoire* — the social-mask written into the role (adolescent slam, teen giggle, coy wife, society hostess). They are signature moves, but they function as transparent self-deception within the drama.

### 5.7 What Fusion is NOT

Fusion is NOT general "tonal variety." Fusion is NOT a transition between states. Fusion is NOT axis-agnostic.

For characters who *don't fake* (honest characters, characters with conviction-rule), Fusions don't fire. A character whose voice rule is "no hedging in prose, the trade is done" has no Fusion repertoire — they don't perform a fake Free pole.

### 5.8 Other "moments of variety" mechanisms (NOT Fusion)

Three distinct mechanisms exist for "moments of texture" that aren't Fusion:

1. **Stress-flip**: Time-stress ↔ Space-stress (Adream); Time ↔ Flow (Stable); etc. Migrates mid-scene without changing Inner Attitude. Lear example.
2. **State-pendulum**: Inner Attitude swings between baseline and Outer Action Attitudes. Not Fusion — orthogonal.
3. **Variation-cycling**: characters use ~3 of the 4 Variations within their Inner Attitude as different "beats." Not a special move, just normal range.

Don't conflate these with Fusion when auditing a character's range.

---

## 6. Externalized Drives — Doing, Passion, Spell, Vision

### 6.1 The four Drives

| Drive | Quality pair | Mental Factor | Subdued (the missing one) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DOING** | exerting / reacting | Sensing | Feeling (Flowless) |
| **PASSION** | constructing / destroying | Feeling | Thinking (Spaceless) |
| **SPELL** | dominating / surrendering | Intuiting | Time |
| **VISION** | ideas / problems | Thinking | Sensing (Weightless) |

Each Drive uses 3 of 4 Motion Factors, defined as much by what's subdued (the missing fourth) as by what's present.

### 6.2 Drive → Inner Attitude mapping (Confluence)

Each Inner Attitude is generated by the meeting of two Drives:

```
STABLE  = DOING + SPELL
ADREAM  = PASSION + SPELL
NEAR    = DOING + PASSION
MOBILE  = PASSION + VISION  (Action only)
REMOTE  = SPELL + VISION    (Action only)
AWAKE   = DOING + VISION    (Action only)
```

Two Drives in a character are NOT equal partners. One dominates, reflecting the character's psychological type — that's the Aspect.

### 6.3 Drive ordering is NOT stable across a scene

> "A Near Character can be Doing/Passion at one moment, but Passion/Doing at another." (p. 552 fn)

The order tracks the active Stress, which tracks circumstance. Big Daddy can be Doing-led at the dinner table and Passion-led at the dam-break.

### 6.4 Action axes (per sub-diagram)

Each Inner Attitude has 4 sub-diagrams (A/B/C/D), one per Stress×Aspect combination. Each gives a distinct Drive pair AND a distinct **Action axis** — the diagonal connecting an Inner Drive corner to an Outer Action Drive corner. This is what manifests on stage.

E.g. Adream:
- A: Spell/Passion → Spell-Doing axis (Claudius)
- B: Spell/Passion → Spell-Vision axis (Hamlet)
- C: Passion/Spell → Passion-Doing axis (Lear, Stanley, Gertrude)
- D: Passion/Spell → Passion-Vision axis (Ophelia, Lovborg, Blanche)

### 6.5 Mirodan's proposed amendment to Spell↔Vision parentage

The canonical assignment (Mental Factor → Drive) creates a paradox:

```
DOING is based on SENSING but has no FEELING
PASSION is based on FEELING but has no THINKING
VISION is based on THINKING but has no SENSING
[paradox] SPELL is based on INTUITING but has no INTUITING
```

Malmgren himself "confesses to be puzzled by this part of Carpenter's book" (p. 537). Mirodan reads Carpenter's later typescript and finds him drifting toward:

> SPELL is generated by THINKING and lacks INTUITING (is Timeless)
> VISION is generated by INTUITING and lacks SENSING (is Weightless)

This restores Jung's original Sensation↔Intuition opposition and reframes "creative thinking" as a function of Intuition (lateral thinking, "seeing things in an unexpected light"). She *proposes amending the system* (p. 540-541). Wittgenstein cited as a Spell exemplar of "cold, logical Spell" generated by systematic thought.

This is a substantial subversion of the simpler Mental-Factor → Drive table on p. 528. Reader needs to know whether the working scheme is Carpenter-canonical or Mirodan-amended.

### 6.6 Vision is omnipresent catalyst

Because every character has a Super-Objective and every Super-Objective is "essentially visionary," Vision must be present (catalytically) in *all* six Attitudes — including the three Weight-bearing (nominally Visionless) ones. (p. 540.) Vision is "an omnipresent catalyst, similar in its effect to the Motion Factor of Time, which is present in all Attitudes."

### 6.7 The whole system is in motion, not static

> "The Inner Attitude, its components, its satellite Attitudes and the 'rockets' of Externalized Drives flying between them are all in perpetual motion. The skill of the actor or director is to use the terms of the system in order to chart the precise location of a given moment in the life of the Character on the map of this constellation." (p. 558)

Reading a character is a dynamic, frame-by-frame act of triangulation.

---

## 7. Lining

### 7.1 Definition

When Stress combines with the *residual* (subdued) Inner Participation in a character, it creates a tiny secondary Action that "lines" the main Action. The lining is **the secret driver behind the visible Outer Action**. Mirodan's coined term (p. 554-555).

### 7.2 The Outer/Lining gap is the engine

The most reusable craft technique in Ch IV. Visible behaviour (Outer Action) is *not* the character — it's the social face. The Lining is the secret driver. The gap creates dramatic life:

- **Mrs Solness** (Master Builder, p. 570-571): Visible Near specificity (cataloguing burnt silk dresses) over a Remote tortured grief. *"a good example of Near enumeration of fact and objects covering a Remote tortured mind."* The Near is the surface; the Remote is the lining. The audience reads the Remote *through* the Near.
- **Hawthorne** (Crucible, p. 565): Near outer (warm magistrate), Stable lining ("dangerous Sustained/Direct certainty") underneath. The Near is the Sunday face; the Stable certainty is what makes the Near brutal.
- **Sir Toby** (Twelfth Night, p. 563): Adream outer (drunk buffoon), Awake lining (calculator scheming to marry Olivia to Sir Andrew for his own Stability). The buffoonery covers the calculator.

### 7.3 How to use Lining

Build the Outer + Lining as a *pair* with the gap creating tension. Don't just name the visible voice — name what's underneath that contradicts it.

For voice writing: every character should have an explicit Lining. The voice on the page is the Outer. The Lining is the secret pressure that gives the voice texture — what the character is *not* saying that nonetheless leaks.

Twins example (p. 555fn): Viola = Passion→Vision, Mobile Outer with Near lining (Space-stressed); Sebastian = Passion→Doing, Near Outer with Mobile lining (Weight-stressed Near). Same play, related characters, opposite Outer/Lining configurations — and the difference is what makes them separable.

---

## 8. Working Actions and the prep hierarchy

### 8.1 The eight Working Actions

Carpenter's definition (p. 340):
> "The eight basic actions, in which all consciously performed activities can be analysed. Compounded of the Motion Factors of Weight, Space, and Time and 'industrial' Free Flow (co-ordination)."

Note: Working Actions use only **3 of 4** Motion Factors. Real Flow (Free/Bound psychological) is reserved as recovery zone between actions. Only "industrial" Free Flow is co-present.

The eight, ordered most-yielding to most-contending:

| Working Action | Weight | Space | Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FLOATING** | light | flexible | sustained |
| **FLICKING** | light | flexible | quick |
| **GLIDING** | light | direct | sustained |
| **DABBING** | light | direct | quick |
| **WRINGING** | strong | flexible | sustained |
| **SLASHING** | strong | flexible | quick |
| **PRESSING** | strong | direct | sustained |
| **PUNCHING** | strong | direct | quick |

(Floating = pure yielding on all three. Punching = pure contending on all three.)

### 8.2 The prep hierarchy (CRITICAL for audit)

> "We cannot carry out a Punching action without first undergoing the inner preparation of Pressing. If I take an axe to chop wood, I first Press to lift the axe above my head and only then release the Punch of the blow. This order cannot be reversed." (p. 347)

The four Quick Working Actions each require their Sustained partner as prep:

| Introvert (Preparation) | → | Extravert (Execution) |
|---|---|---|
| Floating | → | Flicking |
| Gliding | → | Dabbing |
| Wringing | → | Slashing |
| Pressing | → | Punching |

**Skip the prep, the action degrades.** A "Punch" with no Pressing buildup is actually a Press. A "Slash" with no Wringing is a Wring. A "Dab" with no Gliding is a Glide.

For per-line audit on the page: a hard-Punch open ("NO at 46.") with no Pressing in the lines before it isn't actually a Punch — it's a Press masquerading. Hard-exit Punches like "Closed." work because the entire post is the prep. The execution is earned by what came before.

### 8.3 Push/pull and pulse

Each Working Action's energy can flow toward OR away from the mover. A Press can be a push or a pull. The energy has a "pulse" like a musical beat — needs asymmetric weighting (push out + relaxation in, OR swing in + relaxation out).

### 8.4 Quantitative variants

Carpenter's measure: under-stressed / normal / stressed / over-stressed. With these, "there are 64 variants of each of eight Working Actions, making a total of 512" (p. 346). The eight are anchors, not bins.

### 8.5 Continuous transitions

> "Transitions which involve the change of only one Element are smooth and perceived as 'realistic'. Those which change two or even three Elements are big acting jumps which 'shock' the audience." (p. 348-349)

Inexperienced actors love Punching↔Floating jumps. Skilled actors use the full scale, like piano keys.

### 8.6 Working Actions in voice

Working Actions are NOT just gestures — they are *heard* in voice as much as seen in body (p. 349). A Slashing can come out as a winding, insistent, drawn "go on!". Inner Working Actions are coloured by inner Flow: Bound Flow makes a Punch aggressive; Free Flow makes it loving/desiring.

For text-on-page work, Working Actions live in the **rhythm and force of the prose** — sentence length, comma weight, period sharpness, repetition shape. A Pressing line is sustained, accumulating, weight-bearing. A Punching line is short, sudden, hard. A Dabbing line is light and quick. Etc.

---

## 9. Subconscious Motifs / Physical Tempo / Shadow Moves — vocabulary

This section disambiguates three terms that earlier readings collapsed.

### 9.1 Subconscious Motif

> "Laban and Malmgren consider that the inner impulse has the same shape as the outer movement and therefore are analysed through the Elements of Inner Participations in the same way that Working Actions can be analysed through the Elements of Motion Factors. However, to emphasize the psychological nature of the inner impulse, and to avoid confusion in the terminology, these inner counterparts of the Working Actions are called SUBCONSCIOUS MOTIFS." (p. 349)

A **Subconscious Motif** is the *inner* (psychological) version of a Working Action. They share names. Working Action = outer 3-axis Motion-Factor cocktail; Subconscious Motif = inner 3-axis Inner-Participation cocktail. Same pattern, different layer.

In Mirodan's per-Attitude tables (e.g. Adream Strong/Bound = "sombre" → Punching/Pressing), the Working-Action pair listed IS the Subconscious Motif pair. Punching/Pressing is *not* "shadow moves" in this column header — it's the Subconscious Motif column.

### 9.2 Physical Tempo

Mirodan does NOT use "Physical Tempo" as a stand-alone capitalised term. The closest concepts:

- **Tempo per Inner Participation** (p. 286): Inner Participations expressed in overall tempo. Tempo at this level is the physical realisation of Deciding (primum mobile).
- **Three tempi** (p. 323-324): Time is all-pervasive, so we should speak of *dynamic* / *intellectual* / *emotional* tempo — one per Mental Factor that lives in time.
- **Working-Action tempo** (pp. 351-352): each Working Action / transitive verb has a "definite tempo" ("to mock" vs "to chastise" vs "to patronise"). Characters defined by dominant Working Action ARE defined by their dominant tempo.

So "Physical Tempo" in Malmgren's vocabulary = the Working-Action signature of the character. Pace, rhythm, and Working-Action are inseparable. Tempo is the audible/visible trace of Deciding modulating the other functions.

### 9.3 Shadow Moves — definitive

The user's earlier reading and mine were both wrong. **Shadow Moves are NOT axis-pairs.** They are involuntary unconscious gestures.

Carpenter (Conflict & Harmony Ch 1, fn p. 355):
> "Our gestures, mannerisms and bodily attitudes are, however, activated by our subconscious minds and have been aptly christened 'Shadow Moves' by Rudolf Laban since they have no immediate practical purpose. Of these we are normally as unaware as we are of our sun-shadow; they are the visible language of a level of our mind which lies below consciousness."

> "Movements by any part of the body performed without conscious volition expressing Inner Attitudes and Externalized Drives." (Glossary p. 4, q. p. 355)

Mirodan (p. 354):
> "I sometimes think of the inner nature of a character as of a primitive organism, a worm or an amoeba. When 'touched' it reacts, vibrating almost imperceptibly as it re-arranges the Elements of its Inner Participations. This inner movement of energy produces involuntary outer motions, 'half-gestures' with no apparent function other than to reflect and release the inner tensions of the Character. A scratch of the head, a flick of the wrist, a narrowing of the eyes and a myriad similar moves have no functional purpose, but communicate to others the inner reaction."

**Crucial mechanic** (p. 355):
> "They are generated directly by the Subconscious Motifs. A Pressing Subconscious Motif, for example, is not a push or pull against an external obstacle, but an inner holding, a contraction of the central body muscles. A Punching Subconscious Motif is also directed inwardly, as an inner 'kick' against or towards the Objective which gave it birth. It is this inner 'holding', 'kicking', 'slashing', etc., which comes out in Shadow Moves."

### 9.4 Shadow Moves are 2-axis fragments

> "Unlike Working Actions, Shadow Moves cannot be fully analysed into Motion Factors because they are incomplete moves, often used as 'steps' between one Working Action and another. They are incomplete in the sense that they are deficient in one or other of the Motion Factors." (p. 356)

So Shadow Moves are 2-axis (one Motion Factor missing), not the full 3-axis Working Action. They are "steps" between Working Actions and reveal "unstressed" / underdeveloped Inner Participations of the character.

### 9.5 Shadow Moves contradict the conscious Action

> "Shadow Moves always reveal the hidden nature and never the acknowledged intentions of the character. Paulina's Super-Objective in the play (the 'Through-Action' of which the character herself is aware) may be defined by the Character as 'to be loved', but the actress can see that at a deeper, hidden level it is closer to 'to control'." (p. 357)

The Shadow Move betrays the secret motivation while verbal Action gives "lip service" to the open wish. **This connects directly to the Outer/Lining gap of §7.** The Shadow Move IS the Lining made visible in micro-gesture.

### 9.6 Chain of revelation

```
1. Mental Factor (latent) — Sensing/Thinking/Intuiting/Feeling
       │
       ▼ catalysed by external Objective
2. Inner Participation (active) — Intending/Attending/Deciding/Adapting
       │
       ▼ cocktailed in 3-axis combination
3. Subconscious Motif (inner, named after the 8 Working Actions)
       │
       ▼ generates BOTH
4a. Working Actions — outer, conscious, functional, transitive-verb-namable
4b. Shadow Moves — outer, involuntary, non-functional, unconscious gestures
                   (and 2-axis fragments, not 3-axis cocktails)
```

Working Actions and Shadow Moves are the two outward expressions of the same inner Subconscious Motif. Working Action = volitional, has purpose. Shadow Move = involuntary, leaks the secret.

---

## 10. Negatives — frozen interplay

### 10.1 The four Negatives

From the Grand Equation (p. 276):

| Inner Participation (Motion Factor) | Negative |
|---|---|
| Intending (Weight) | **Heavy** |
| Attending (Space) | **Adrift** |
| Deciding (Time) | **Indecisive** |
| Adapting (Flow) | **Irrelated** |

### 10.2 Negatives are NOT axis-off

> "in Negative states, the interplay between the Elements is cancelled, NOT the Elements themselves." (p. 334)

A Heavy character is aware she ought to be Strong-Intending but cannot summon the energy. "I just can't, I can't be bothered." Heavy = the unresolvable cramp-vs-collapse fight between Light and Strong, frozen in static equilibrium. Same logic per axis.

### 10.3 Per-Negative gloss

**HEAVY** (Weight) — p. 333:
> "The negatively neutral and impotent quality of Weight in which the interplay of Light and Strong is cancelled by an inertia which negates the receiving and transmitting intentions of Sensing." ("Book" p. 14)

Depressed people: beard grows, dishes pile up. Blanche du Bois takes baths constantly because circulation has frozen.

**ADRIFT** (Space) — p. 335:
> "The negatively neutral quality of Space in which the interplay of Flexible and Direct is cancelled by a disorientation which negates the reflective (concave) and attending (convex) aspects of Thinking." ("Book" p. 14)

Blank expression. Bemused, lost. Hearing but not listening.

**INDECISIVE** (Time) — p. 335-336:
> "The negatively neutral dream quality of Time in which the interplay of Sustained and Quick is cancelled by a timelessness which negates the relating of past, present and future in the decisions of Intuiting." ("Book" p. 14)

Floats around, no past memories, no future hopes. Affects "dreamy" expression thinking it makes him interesting — actually makes him "plain stupid."

**IRRELATED** (Flow) — p. 336:
> "The negatively neutral and frozen quality of Flow in which the interplay of Free and Bound is cancelled by an emotional fixity which negates the extraverted and introverted aspects of the adapting and relating of Feeling." ("Book" p. 14)

Emotional deep freeze. The proverbial commuter reading his paper on the train.

### 10.4 Per-state Negative pairs

Each Inner Attitude has TWO Negatives — the Negatives of its two active Motion Factors:

| State | Active factors | Negatives |
|---|---|---|
| Stable | Weight + Space | Heavy + Adrift |
| Mobile | Time + Flow | Indecisive + Irrelated |
| Near | Weight + Time | Heavy + Indecisive |
| Remote | Space + Flow | Adrift + Irrelated |
| Awake | Space + Time | Adrift + Indecisive |
| Adream | Weight + Flow | Heavy + Irrelated |

### 10.5 Negatives as character-failure mode

Yieldsalot-style "drifty hedge voice" reads through Negatives, not through Light-baseline. A Near character stuck in Heavy is a character aware they should commit Strong but can't — the paralysis IS the voice. Don't model failure modes as "the axis is off"; model them as "the axis is suspended."

---

## 11. Mirodan's analytical method

Reverse-engineered from how she works each character (Ch IV).

**Step 1 — Locate the dominant Inner Attitude.** Name first, one sentence of justification. "What does this character DO best, fundamentally?"

**Step 2 — Specify Stress and Aspect** to pick the sub-diagram (A/B/C/D). Both must be named to pin down which of the four sub-diagrams applies.

**Step 3 — Read off Character Drives + Action axis** from the diagram. The sub-diagram does the work.

**Step 4 — Specify Outer Action and its Lining (Inner Action).** The visible behaviour gets named. The Lining is the secret driver behind it. Every Outer Action has a Lining.

**Step 5 — Note polar visitations / fluctuations.** Where does the character travel under pressure? What triggers it? Cordelia/Lear "change places"; Creon collapses to Mobile; Olivia visits Remote at first encounter with Cesario. Map the trajectory.

**Step 6 — Cite textual + production evidence.** Every reading is grounded in a play-text reference, a production memory, or a line.

**Step 7 — Hold readings in productive ambiguity when warranted.** Iago could be Near or Adream. Olivia fits both Remote A and B. Zarastro is C-or-D. Multiple readings are *fine* and often correct.

**Step 8 — Read characters relationally, not in isolation.** The cast is a system. Iago's Inner Attitude depends on what Othello is.

This is the meta-template for our own character-voice work.

---

## 12. Worked character analyses

This section consolidates Mirodan's ~50 worked readings as a queryable reference. The most-instructive 6 cases are expanded; the rest are tabulated.

### 12.1 Expanded cases

#### Hawthorne (the Judge) — *The Crucible* — Stable A
Reading (p. 565):
> "He has the cool reserve and self confidence of the colonial establishment. He is enclosing Stable ('A'). If he were penetrating he would not be so stuck in his ways. He feels solid, lacks flexibility. He is predominantly Strong/Direct. He is Time-stressed more than Flow-stressed — he has no Adapting, remains always firm. Hence his outer Actions are Near and Awake. The Nearness is his strongest Attitude — mostly 'warm' — Strong/Sustained — out of a dangerous Sustained/Direct ('certain'): his working Actions are mostly Pressing and Gliding. His diagram is therefore 'A': Doing/Spell with a Doing-Passion Action."

This is the longest worked example. Full chain: Aspect → Stress → Outer Actions → Lining → specific Working Actions → Action axis.

Use as a template. The character spec produces named Working Actions.

#### Mrs Solness — *The Master Builder* — Remote from Adream, Diagram D
Reading (p. 570-571):
> "Mrs. Solness is a tortured mind, Remote from Adream. The Near Action then comes through — the Near connection to the details of the fire: 'All the old portraits on the walls were burnt. And so were all the old silk dresses. They'd been in the family for generations.' — is a good example of Near enumeration of fact and objects covering a Remote tortured mind. She then comes back to a Remote Outer Action with her talk of dolls — symbolic of the children who were burnt. Her Remote Outer Action is Vision-Passion ('D')."

The clearest demonstration of Lining in practice: visible Near specificity (lists of objects) on top of Remote inner state. Audience reads the Remote *through* the Near.

#### Lear — *King Lear* — Adream C, with the swing of the pendulum
Reading (pp. 470-473):
The curse is Adream Strong/Bound (Punching/Pressing). Beforehand he greets Albany with Direct Attending → Outer Stable/Remote (turning rage cold). Then mid-scene Stress migrates: Time-stress → Space-stress → back. Lear oscillates: Time-stressed fury → Space-stressed reflection → fury → Stable inner / Mobile outer.

Mirodan: "the swing of the pendulum." Stress flip mid-scene. Stress is BOTH baseline AND episodic in the same speech.

This is the canonical case for Stress-flip mechanics.

#### Hamlet — *Hamlet* — Adream B (radiating, Space-stressed)
Drives: Spell/Passion, Action axis Spell-Vision. The Hamlet-grouping (p. 561) lists similar characters: Richard II, the Duke in *Measure for Measure*, Melancholy Jaques in *As You Like It*. Different actors choose different Hamlets — Olivier was radiating Adream; Smoktunovski and Finney chose enclosing types.

Productive ambiguity is the right answer (cf. §11 Step 7).

#### Stanley Kowalski — *Streetcar* — Adream C (enclosing, Time-stressed)
Drives: Passion/Spell, Passion-Doing axis. Same Diagram as Lear. The lower-status Time-stress fits Stanley's class position (cf. §1 status grammar).

#### Blanche du Bois — *Streetcar* — Adream D → Mobile B (terminal flip)
Reading (p. 567): "From the start we can see that she abhors Weight, she does not feel any attraction towards men, only towards boyish, androgynous youths. She is Weight-stressed radiating (aspires to sensuality but can not achieve it)." She "evaporates into Mobility" at the end.

The trajectory IS the character. Adream radiating Time-stressed throughout most of the play; Mobile state at the asylum.

|---|---|---|---|---|

| Claudius | Hamlet | Adream | A (encl/Space, Spell-Doing) | 561 |

### 12.3 Patterns across the cast

- **Most plays cluster around Adream**. The core triangle of dramatic tension is usually three Adream characters (different Diagrams) plus a Near "fourth element" that gates or troubles the Adream constellation. Streetcar = Stella/Stanley/Blanche (Adream A/C/D) + Mitch (Near D). Miss Julie = same pattern.
- **One Stable per play**, typically. The Stable is structurally singular (p. 575). Patriarchal/establishment figure who anchors the constellation.
- **Mobile and Remote are reached through trauma**. Crisis triggers polar-visit. Olivia's Cesario scene; Creon's son's death; Blanche's evaporation; Cordelia's army return; Yelena's soliloquy.
- **The opposite-pair has a secret longing for the opposite** (p. 575 fn): "Yat Malmgren makes a similar point about Electra and Clytemnestra — the oppressor has a secret longing to take the victim's place and vice-versa." Polar-flip is mutual and secret.

---

## 13. Synthesis — rules for character voice writing

YOUR distillation of the load-bearing rules for someone building a Laban-grounded character voice spec.

### Rule 1 — Name all six dimensions explicitly

A spec that names only "Adream" or "Stable" is doing 1/6 of the work. Always specify:
1. Inner Attitude
2. Aspect (enclosing/penetrating/circumscribing/radiating)
3. Dominant Stress (with pole — Bound vs Free, Quick vs Sustained, etc.)
4. Outer Action Attitude(s) — derived from 1 + 3
5. Lining — what the Outer covers
6. Working Actions — 1-3 specific gestures the voice defaults to

### Rule 2 — Baseline must be Stable, Near, or Adream

These are the only three with Weight (Sensing) active. Mobile, Awake, Remote can never be a character's baseline. They are Action Attitudes — outer projections under stress. If your character's baseline doesn't have Weight, they're not human.

### Rule 3 — Honour the opposite-pair hard rule

Stable↔Mobile, Near↔Remote, Awake↔Adream are mutually exclusive. A character anchored to one cannot hold its opposite. Polar-flip moments are *narrative pivots* — the dam breaking. Don't confuse a flip with normal range.

### Rule 4 — Build the Outer/Lining gap into the spec

The visible voice should *contradict or complicate* the Lining. Mrs Solness's Near catalogue of burnt silks over Remote tortured grief. Sir Toby's drunken buffoon over Awake calculation. Hawthorne's Near warmth over Stable certainty. The gap creates dramatic life. If your character has only an Outer with no Lining, they're flat.

### Rule 5 — Fusion is for characters who fake. Honest characters don't Fusion

Fusions are transparent self-deception (Free pole fake, Bound pole truth). They live only on Flow and only in Adream/Mobile/Remote. If your character's voice rule is "no hedging in prose, the trade is done," they're not a Fusion character. Don't write fake-Free moments for honest characters.

### Rule 6 — Respect the prep hierarchy in line-by-line audits

Punches need Pressing-prep. Slashes need Wringing-prep. Dabs need Gliding-prep. Flicks need Floating-prep. Skip the prep, the action degrades. A "Punch" with no Pressing buildup is actually a Press. Hard-exit Punches like "Closed." work because the entire post is the prep.

### Rule 7 — Tempo is the engine, not just an axis

Time/Deciding is *primum mobile*. Tempo pre-decides which function engages. Set the character's dominant tempo (dynamic / intellectual / emotional) and let the other axes ride on it.

### Rule 8 — Stress is dominant baseline AND episodic

Name the dominant Stress. But the non-dominant is available for "beats" within scenes. The swing of the pendulum is signature, not drift. Build space for Stress-flip moments — the secondary axis migrating creates real-time character life.

### Rule 9 — Status grammar matters

Time-stress reads adolescent / lower. Space-stress reads mature / higher. Flow-stress reads emotional / sociable. Pick the Stress that matches the character's social position. Don't accidentally pair an aristocratic character with a Time-stress and get class confusion.

### Rule 10 — Productive ambiguity is allowed

If a character genuinely lives between two Diagrams (Malvolio = B+C; Olivia in Cesario scene = Remote A+B; The Duke = Adream B / Stable D), name the pair. Don't force a single quadrant. Real characters straddle.

### Rule 11 — Negatives are paralysis, not absence

Heavy = aware should be Strong but cannot mobilise. Adrift = aware should be Direct but disoriented. Indecisive = timeless paralysis. Irrelated = emotional deep freeze. If your character is failing on an axis, model the failure as suspended interplay between poles, not as absence. The frozen Strong/Light battle is the voice.

### Rule 12 — The roster is a system, not a stack

Place characters across the Attitude grid intentionally. Stable is structurally singular per play. Adream characters cluster. Near serves as the "fourth element" that troubles the cluster. Mobile/Remote/Awake are states the cluster visits under stress. When designing a multi-character roster, distribute: one Stable, several Adream-different-diagrams, one or two Near, and reserve the Action-only states as moments — don't spec a baseline-Mobile character.

### Rule 13 — Character is a trajectory, not a snapshot

The Inner Attitude is stable through the play. The Stress, Aspect, Outer Actions, polar-visits all evolve. Encode a *trajectory* — start state, trigger conditions, end state. Cordelia/Lear change places. Blanche evaporates. Creon collapses. The arc IS the character.

### Rule 14 — Quote the source verbatim where the language is precise

Mirodan's specific terms (Aspect, Lining, Fusion, Subconscious Motif, Working Action, Variation, Drive, Action axis) carry exact meanings. Don't paraphrase loosely — the framework breaks down if you swap "shadow move" for "subconscious motif" or "polar visitation" for "Fusion." Use her vocabulary or invent new vocabulary explicitly.

### Rule 15 — Validator-side, not generator-side

Most LLM-character-voice failures come from claiming facts you didn't verify or violating spec rules you didn't audit. The right architecture is: generator stays cheap and naive, validator does the heavy lifting. The validator's job: per-line shorthand audit (can you name the Variation+Subconscious Motif?), prep hierarchy check, register coherence, factual claim extraction with web verification, stale-data flag. Same validator runs across the whole roster — only the character spec changes.

## End notes

**OCR caveats**: The PDF has OCR errors on "Adream" (often "Adreaiu"/"Adreazu"), "Iago" (always "lago"), and Effort-shorthand inside diagram quadrants. The structural information is intact; spelling slips don't change the readings.

**Mirodan's emendation status**: Her proposed Spell↔Vision swap (Spell = Thinking-generated, Vision = Intuiting-generated, restoring Jung's Sensation/Intuition opposition) is unconfirmed by the rest of the system but is internally coherent. When applying the framework, decide whether to use the Carpenter-canonical or Mirodan-amended Drive parentage. For our character voice work the difference matters for Spell characters specifically.

**Unfinished**: Mirodan's Ch I extract notes the original shadow-move definition "for some reason does not feature in the latest version of Yat Malmgren's 'Book'" (fn p. 355). The system has had multiple revisions; some terms may have drifted in later editions. When in doubt, this reference uses Mirodan's 1997 Vol 2 readings.
